{"step_by_step_thinking": "The patient is a 28-year-old with a history of neurocysticercosis who developed generalized peeling of the skin after starting anti-epileptic medications. The peeling is widespread except for the palms and soles, and it has been ongoing for about a month. Given the timeline and the fact that the patient is on anti-epileptics, the most probable diagnosis is a drug-induced reaction. Among the options, fixed drug eruption (A) is a localized reaction that typically presents as a persistent, round, erythematous lesion at the site of previous drug application. Pemphigus (B) and Steven Johnson syndrome (C) are autoimmune blistering diseases that do not typically present with generalized peeling. Toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN, D) is a severe form of drug reaction that causes extensive skin detachment, which does not match the description of generalized peeling. Therefore, the most probable diagnosis is fixed drug eruption.", "answer_choice": "A"}
